headline_metrics.nii: NII X markets ~$96.5B (FY2026 guide); total NII ~$105.5B (FY2026 guide)
headline_metrics.nco_rate: Card NCO rate ~3.2% (FY2026 guide); net charge-offs $2.4B in Q2

nim_nii.summary: JPM raised its full-year 2026 NII X markets guide from $95B to ~$96.5B and total NII to ~$105.5B, with markets NII guided higher to ~$9B. The primary driver of the upward revision was stronger deposit balances (both wholesale and consumer) and a favorable mix shift, with higher short- and long-end rates providing a secondary boost. Management also noted consumer deposit betas have outperformed models, adding incremental sensitivity.
nim_nii.quote: just mechanically in terms of the drivers of the upward revision, the biggest single factor is deposit balances, I would say across both wholesale and consumer both the overall quantum of it, but also makes shift inside of that in favor of slightly higher margin overall then rates are a little bit higher than when we previously guided both in the short end and in the back end.
nim_nii.speaker: Jeremy Barnum, Chief Financial Officer

credit_normalization.summary: Credit performance came in better than expected: JPM reported $2.4B in net charge-offs with a $149M reserve build, and revised its full-year card NCO rate guide down to ~3.2%. Delinquencies are running a little lower than expected across FICO cohorts, and management attributed the resilience squarely to a still-solid labor market.
credit_normalization.quote: we now expect the card net charge-off rate to be approximately 3.2% reflecting better than expected consumer credit performance.
credit_normalization.speaker: Jeremy Barnum, Chief Financial Officer

cre_reserves.summary: CRE reserves were not specifically broken out or discussed in the call. The firm reported a modest net reserve build of $149M at the firm-wide level, but management did not attribute any portion to CRE or office CRE specifically, nor did analysts press on it.

deposit_costs.summary: Management noted that consumer deposit betas have outperformed (i.e., come in better than modeled), meaning deposit costs have risen less than expected. Yield-seeking flows remain a factor and a modest risk in a higher-for-longer rate environment, but overall deposit cost dynamics are in line with or better than prior expectations. Management flagged convexity risk if rates move meaningfully higher but said that inflection point has not yet arrived.
deposit_costs.quote: our actual sensitivity is a little bit more than the ear suggests right now because of the outperformance of the consumer betas relative to the model and that difference is probably disproportionately in the front end.
deposit_costs.speaker: Jeremy Barnum, Chief Financial Officer

capital_return.summary: The Board intends to raise the quarterly dividend to $1.65/share in Q3 2026. CET1 stood at 14.1%, down 20 bps QoQ as net income was more than offset by higher RWA and capital distributions. Management indicated they have excess capital but are increasingly inclined to deploy it organically or inorganically rather than via buybacks at current elevated valuations (~3x tangible book), and pushed back on the concept of buybacks as 'returning money to shareholders.' On regulation, Dimon and Barnum advocated strongly for fixing Basel III methodology flaws (double-count in market risk and operating risk capital, G-SIB surcharge calibration, short-term wholesale funding treatment).
capital_return.quote: As you saw in our CCAR press release in June, the Board intends to increase the quarterly dividend to $1.65 per share effective in the third quarter.
capital_return.speaker: Jeremy Barnum, Chief Financial Officer

loan_growth.summary: Loan growth was organic and broad-based: AWM cited higher loan balances, CCB highlighted deposit and loan balance growth, and wholesale deposit growth was described as strong and ahead of expectations partly because lending is creating deposits (especially in the MDFI/data center space). Management explicitly framed organic growth as the primary capital deployment priority across every business line. No acquisition-driven loan growth was cited.
loan_growth.quote: some of it is also the kind of lending environment, particularly the sort of MDFI space and a lot of the data center stuff like however you look at it, you have a little bit of the dynamic of loans creating deposits and that's going to disproportionately show up in wholesale.
loan_growth.speaker: Jeremy Barnum, Chief Financial Officer

macro_outlook.summary: Management described the current environment as 'getting close to as good as it gets' — healthy labor market, strong consumer spend, resilient credit — but explicitly cautioned they don't know how long it will last. Dimon pointed to geopolitical risks, elevated global deficits, and a complex, 'exuberant' market. Barnum flagged potential consumer fragility if rates rise meaningfully, noting the convexity risk in deposit betas and a cohort experiencing negative real wage growth as watch areas.
macro_outlook.quote: It's getting close to as good as it gets. We just don't know how long it's going to last.
macro_outlook.speaker: Jamie Dimon, Chairman and CEO

notable_qa.summary: Mike Mayo pressed management on why buybacks make sense at ~3x tangible book when the environment could deteriorate, and whether negative operating leverage was acceptable. Dimon pushed back sharply, rejecting the framing of buybacks as 'returning money to shareholders,' explaining it is an investment decision, and that with global capital deployment opportunities (infrastructure, AI, Europe, organic growth) the firm would rather deploy excess capital productively. He also flatly dismissed perpetual operating leverage as a 'crazy notion' for a firm already generating exceptional returns.
notable_qa.quote: buying back stock is not returning money to shareholders. You're making an investment decision, you're not making a return money to shareholders decisions.
notable_qa.speaker: Jamie Dimon, Chairman and CEO
